conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have a child - Alex, who is 16 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to understand their current situation.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex actually just left school recently, but they've signed up with the local careers service to figure out what they want to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know that Alex has registered with the careers service after leaving education.

Just to clarify - approximately how long ago did Alex leave school or training? Was it within the last 20 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's been quite recent - they're still within that period since leaving school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Now I need to understand your living situation to complete the assessment.

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or are you the only person responsible for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. I'm the only one responsible for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit based on what you've told me.

Based on the official guidance, here's my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news!** You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Even though Alex has recently left school, the rules allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service (or the armed services). Since Alex has registered with the local careers service and is still within that 20-week period, you qualify for Child Benefit.

**Important things to note:**
- This 20-week extension gives Alex time to decide their next steps
- If Alex returns to approved education or training before the 20 weeks ends, your Child Benefit can continue as long as they remain in approved education or training and are under 20
- If Alex starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week, or starts claiming certain benefits in their own right, your Child Benefit would stop

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit entitlement?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

circumstances: Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and has recently left education. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Alex has registered with the local careers service and is still within the 20-week period since leaving school. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) with Alex, is responsible for Alex, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. Therefore, Alex meets the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit during this 20-week extension period.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 16-year-old child Alex. Although Alex has left education, they registered with the local careers service and are still within the 20-week extension period allowed for 16-17 year olds who leave education/training and register with a careers service or armed services. The claimant lives in the UK with Alex, is solely responsible for them, and no one else is claiming for Alex.

The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) with Alex, is responsible for Alex, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. Therefore, Alex meets the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit during this 20-week extension period.
